py-intro.title: Introduction to Python
py-intro.explanation:
Python is a popular programming language used for web development, data analysis, automation, and more. It is easy to read and write, which makes it a great first language for beginners.

You learn Python first to focus on logic and problem solving instead of worrying about complicated syntax.
py-intro.example:
# This code prints a friendly message
print("Welcome to Python!")

# Python uses simple English-like statements
name = "Aisha"
print("Hello,", name)
py-intro.tip: Python is designed to be very readable. Use indentation and simple naming to keep your code clean.
py-intro.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — missing quotes around text
message = Hello Python

# ✅ Correct
message = "Hello Python"
py-intro.practice: Write a short Python program that prints your name and a sentence about why you want to learn coding.

py-setup.title: Setup & Installation
py-setup.explanation:
Setting up Python on your computer lets you run Python programs locally. You will install the interpreter and verify that Python works through a simple test program.

A correct setup is the first step before you start learning real Python code.
py-setup.example:
# Verify Python in a terminal or command prompt
# Type the following command:
python --version

# Then create a file named hello.py with:
print("Hello from Python")

# Run it with:
python hello.py
py-setup.tip:
Use the latest stable Python version, and remember to add Python to your system PATH if your installer asks.
If your OS already has Python, use the same command to verify the version.
py-setup.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — saving a file with the wrong extension
hello.txt

# ✅ Correct
hello.py
py-setup.practice: Install Python on your computer, create a file called hello.py, and run it from the terminal to confirm the setup works.

py-syntax.title: Python Syntax
py-syntax.explanation:
Python syntax is the set of rules for writing code. It is often easier than many other languages because it uses indentation instead of braces and reads like plain English.

Understanding syntax helps you write correct programs and avoid simple errors.
py-syntax.example:
# Python uses indentation for blocks
if 5 > 2:
    print("Five is greater than two")

# Comments start with #
# This is a comment
py-syntax.tip: Indentation is not optional in Python. Use the same number of spaces for each block. A common choice is 4 spaces.
py-syntax.practice: Write an if statement that checks whether a number is positive and prints a message if it is.

py-types.title: Python Data Types
py-types.explanation:
Data types describe the kind of values you store in variables. Python supports numbers, text, lists, booleans, and more.

Knowing data types lets you choose the right kind of value for each task.
py-types.example:
name = "Lina"      # String
age = 24             # Integer
price = 12.99        # Float
is_active = True     # Boolean

print(type(name))
print(type(age))
py-types.tip: Python figures out the type automatically. Use type() to inspect values when you are learning.
py-types.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — adding text and numbers directly
result = "Age: " + 20

# ✅ Correct
result = "Age: " + str(20)
py-types.practice: Create one variable of each type: string, integer, float, and boolean. Print each variable and its type.

py-ops.title: Python Operators
py-ops.explanation:
Operators perform actions with values. Python has math operators, comparison operators, and logical operators.

Operators are the building blocks for expressions and decisions.
py-ops.example:
x = 10
y = 3
print(x + y)   # 13
print(x * y)   # 30
print(x > y)   # True
print(x == y)  # False
py-ops.tip: Remember that == checks for equality, while = assigns a value. Also use parentheses to group expressions clearly.
py-ops.practice: Write an expression that compares two numbers and prints whether the first is larger, smaller, or equal.

py-io.title: Python Input & Output
py-io.explanation:
Input and output let your programs interact with users. Use input() to read text and print() to show results.

This makes programs feel interactive instead of just running silently.
py-io.example:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello, " + name + "!")

age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print("You are", age, "years old.")
py-io.tip: input() always returns text. Convert it with int() or float() before using it in math operations.
py-io.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — adding user input to numbers without converting
value = input("Enter a number: ")
print(value + 5)

# ✅ Correct
value = int(input("Enter a number: "))
print(value + 5)
py-io.practice: Ask the user for two numbers, add them together, and print the sum.

py-cond.title: Python Conditions
py-cond.explanation:
Conditions let your program make decisions. You use if, elif, and else to run different code depending on whether a statement is true or false.

This is how programs choose different paths and behave more like real applications.
py-cond.tip: Each if, elif, and else block must use the same indentation, and only one block runs when conditions are checked from top to bottom.
py-cond.practice: Write a program that asks for a temperature and prints whether it is cold, warm, or hot.

py-func.title: Python Functions
py-func.explanation:
Functions group code into reusable blocks. You define a function once and call it whenever you need the same behavior.

This keeps your code shorter, easier to read, and easier to fix.
py-func.example:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello, " + name + "!")

greet("Mira")
greet("Ali")
py-func.tip: Name functions with action words, like calculate_total or show_message. Keep each function focused on one task.
py-func.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — forgetting parentheses when calling a function
def say_hi():
    print("Hi")

say_hi  # missing ()

# ✅ Correct
say_hi()
py-func.practice: Create a function that takes a number and prints whether it is even or odd. Call it with at least two numbers.

py-list.title: Python Lists
py-list.explanation:
Lists let you store multiple values in one variable. You can add, remove, and access items by their position.

Lists are one of the most common ways to work with groups of data in Python.
py-list.tip: List indexes start at 0, so the first item is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on.
py-list.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — using a negative index by mistake
print(fruits[3])  # error if list has only 3 items

# ✅ Correct
print(fruits[2])  # last item in a 3-item list
py-list.practice: Make a list of your favorite foods, print the first item, and add one more food to the list.

py-dict.title: Python Dictionaries
py-dict.explanation:
Dictionaries store values using keys instead of positions. They are useful when you want to look up information by a label.

This makes it easy to organize related data like names and scores.
py-dict.tip: Use meaningful keys like name, score, or email. Dictionaries are not ordered by position in older Python versions, so use keys, not indexes.
py-dict.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — using a number index with a dictionary
print(student[0])

# ✅ Correct
print(student["name"])
py-dict.practice: Create a dictionary for a book with title, author, and year. Print the author and update the year.

py-vars.title: Variables in Python
py-vars.explanation:
A variable is a named container that stores a value in memory. In Python, you don't need to declare the type — Python figures it out automatically!

Think of a variable like a labeled box: you put something in it, give it a name, and use that name to find it later.
py-vars.example:
# Creating variables
name = "Alice"       # String variable
age = 20             # Integer variable
gpa = 3.8            # Float variable
is_student = True    # Boolean variable

# Using variables
print(name)      # Output: Alice
print(age)       # Output: 20
print(gpa)       # Output: 3.8
py-vars.tip: Variable names should be descriptive. Use snake_case (my_name) not camelCase in Python. Variable names cannot start with a number!
py-vars.mistake:
# ❌ Wrong — variable name starts with number
1name = "Alice"

# ❌ Wrong — using spaces in name
my name = "Alice"

# ✅ Correct
my_name = "Alice"
first_name = "Alice"
py-vars.practice: Create 3 variables: your name, your age, and your favorite programming language. Print all three on separate lines.

py-loops.title: Loops in Python
py-loops.explanation:
Loops allow you to repeat a block of code multiple times without writing it over and over. Python has two main loop types:

• for loop — repeats for each item in a sequence
• while loop — repeats as long as a condition is true
py-loops.example:
# for loop — count from 0 to 4
for i in range(5):
    print(i)
# Output: 0  1  2  3  4

# for loop — iterate over a list
fruits = ["apple", "mango", "banana"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print("I love", fruit)

# while loop — count up
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print("Count:", count)
    count += 1    # Don't forget to increment!
py-loops.tip: Always make sure a while loop has a way to stop! If the condition never becomes False, your program will run forever (infinite loop).
py-loops.mistake:
# ❌ Infinite loop — missing increment!
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    # forgot: count += 1

# ✅ Correct
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1
py-loops.practice: Write a loop that prints all even numbers from 2 to 20. Use both a for loop and a while loop version.
